did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning (DL) models, specifically SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16, to classify skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion) using the ISIC dataset. The study employed supervised learning and data augmentation techniques to obtain classification results evaluated by accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the classification of skin diseases using nine specific deep learning models (SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16) on the ISIC dataset. The study includes a 'Material and Method' section detailing data augmentation, training hyperparameters, and evaluation metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1-score), followed by a 'Results' section reporting specific performance metrics for each model. This structure and content indicate original empirical research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Diseases with Different Deep Learning Models and Comparison of the Performances of the Models
brief_summary: This study evaluated the classification performance of nine deep learning models for five skin diseases: Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion. The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset, expanding the data via augmentation techniques such as reflection, inversion, and rotation to create a total of 3,548 training images. The models assessed included SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16, all trained using supervised learning with transfer learning. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score metrics derived from confusion matrices. The results indicated that ResNet-18 achieved the highest accuracy at 98.06%, while Vgg-16 obtained the highest F1-score at 97.41%. The high success rates suggest that these deep learning systems are effective for aiding in the early diagnosis and treatment of skin diseases.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using nine deep learning models (SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16) on the ISIC dataset. The task involves assigning input images to one of five discrete classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing images of skin diseases. The study utilized images from this dataset, specifically focusing on five classes of skin diseases. The original dataset was expanded using data augmentation techniques (reflection, inversion, 90-degree rotation, and 180-degree rotation) to increase the number of samples.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, Vgg-16) via supervised learning and transfer learning. 80% of the augmented images were reserved for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. 20% of the images were reserved for testing. Each model was trained three times and tested after each training, with average results used for final evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate average performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1-score) for each model to mitigate variance from random weight initialization and data splitting.
To derive classification metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-score) by counting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each model.
To report the final performance of each deep learning model by averaging results from three separate training/testing runs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive statistics (averages of accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score) derived from confusion matrices across three training iterations.

ISIC Dataset (5-class classification: Ac.evaluation_setting: Supervised learning with transfer learning. Each model trained 3 times; results are averages of these runs. Metrics derived from confusion matrices.
ISIC Dataset (5-class classification: Ac.notes: ResNet-18 achieved the highest accuracy (98.06%). Vgg-16 achieved the highest F1-score (97.41%). The performance range for accuracy was 94.84% to 98.06%, and for F1-score was 95.16% to 97.41%.

architecture_used.main_model: Nine Deep Learning models: SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used; weights were determined by training on the dataset (supervised learning). Specific pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) are not explicitly named, but 'transfer learning' is cited as the method for weight optimization.

architecture_details.general_architecture_notes: Models utilize convolutional layers for feature extraction, pooling layers to reduce image size, ReLU activation functions, dropout layers to reduce memorization, normalization layers for standardization, fully connected layers to combine features, and Softmax layers for probabilistic estimation/classification.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (standard cross-entropy implied by Softmax/Classification layer)
training_details.training_schedule: Each model was trained three times and tested after each training. Average results of the three runs were used for final evaluation. 80% of augmented data used for training, 20% for testing.

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax layer for probabilistic estimation followed by a Classification layer

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Reflection, inversion, 90-degree rotation, and 180-degree rotation to expand the dataset (quadrupling the number of images).

Image resizing: Input images were resized to specific dimensions for each model (227x227 for SqueezeNet and AlexNet; 224x224 for GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16).
Normalization: Image data was standardized via a normalization layer within the deep learning architecture.

conclusions: Deep learning models can effectively classify five specific skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion) with high performance, suggesting their utility in computer-aided diagnosis systems for early detection and treatment.
Among the nine evaluated models (SqueezeNet, AlexNet, GoogleNet, Vgg-19, ResNet101, DenseNet201, ResNet-50, ResNet-18, and Vgg-16), ResNet-18 achieved the highest accuracy at 98.06%.
Vgg-16 achieved the highest F1-score at 97.41% among the tested models.

The performance of different deep learning models varies depending on the dataset and study area, although all tested models demonstrated high success rates (accuracy range: 94.84% to 98.06%; F1-score range: 95.16% to 97.41%).
Data augmentation techniques (reflection, inversion, 90-degree rotation, and 180-degree rotation) were effective in expanding the dataset to improve training performance.
The study confirms that deep learning systems are promising tools for assisting dermatologists in the diagnosis and treatment processes of skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only five specific skin disease classes (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion) from the ISIC dataset, excluding critical malignancies like melanoma or basal cell carcinoma, which limits generalizability to broader dermatological diagnosis.

The dataset is severely imbalanced, with Pigmented Benign Keratosis having 462 original images compared to only 77 for Seborrheic Keratosis; this imbalance may bias model performance towards the majority class.

Data augmentation (reflection, inversion, 90/180-degree rotation) was used to quadruple the dataset size, but the paper does not report whether augmentation was applied only to the training set or if it risks data leakage into the test set.
The paper does not specify the source of the pre-trained weights used for transfer learning (e.g., ImageNet), making reproducibility difficult.

No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the same ISIC dataset it was trained on, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The study lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between models, relying solely on average metrics from three training runs.

The reported accuracy range (94.84% to 98.06%) is very high for a multi-class medical classification task with a small dataset (3,548 total images), suggesting potential over-optimistic results or insufficient regularization against overfitting.

The paper does not discuss clinical deployment challenges, such as inference speed, computational resource requirements, or integration into clinical workflows, despite claiming utility for early diagnosis.
